threats.title: eq() Uses Bitwise Comparison Failing for Same Value with Different M/L Encoding
threats.hypothesis_description: The eq() function performs a raw uint256 bitwise comparison (`packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)`) instead of mathematical value comparison. Two packedFloats representing the exact same mathematical value but encoded with different mantissa sizes (M=38 digits, L=72 digits) will have completely different bit patterns and eq() returns false. This violates reflexivity-under-value-equivalence for any protocol comparing computed results.
threats.investigation_rationale: The same number can be encoded as M (mantissa 10^37, exponent e) or L (mantissa 10^71, exponent e-34). These have different bit patterns due to different mantissa, exponent, and L-flag values. Any equality check between values from different computation paths will fail.

proof_of_signal.pattern: eq() implementation is `retVal = packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)` - pure bitwise comparison

preconditions: Two packedFloats represent the same mathematical value
They have different M/L encodings (one 38-digit, one 72-digit mantissa)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Bitwise uint256 comparison instead of value-based comparison
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: 1.0 encoded as M-size (mantissa=10^37, rawExp=8155) vs L-size (mantissa=10^71, rawExp=8121) produces completely different uint256 values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should normalize both to same size before comparing, or compare decoded (mantissa, exponent) pairs

analysis_questions: How frequently do arithmetic operations produce cross-M/L encodings for the same value?
Does the auto-scaling at MAXIMUM_EXPONENT boundary create natural M/L encoding divergence?
Would fixing eq() also require fixing le() and ge()?
validation_requirements: Create toPackedFloat(1, 0) and toPackedFloat(int(10^71), -71) and verify eq() returns false
Verify both decode to the same mathematical value
Test with values produced by actual arithmetic operations

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A protocol checks `eq(currentRate, targetRate)` to determine if an interest rate has been reached. If currentRate is computed through division (producing L encoding) and targetRate was stored from a different calculation (M encoding), the equality check never triggers even when rates are identical, preventing rate adjustments or triggering incorrect fallback behavior.
prerequisites: Protocol uses eq() for value matching
Compared values originate from different computation paths

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Failed equality checks bypass protocol invariants and conditional logic
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users affected by equality-gated protocol logic

threats.title: ln() Silently Accepts Negative Inputs Instead of Reverting
threats.hypothesis_description: The ln() function extracts the mantissa using `and(input, MANTISSA_MASK)` which strips the sign bit (bit 240) without checking it. For negative inputs, ln() silently computes ln(|input|) and returns a valid positive result. Natural logarithm is mathematically undefined for negative real numbers. This masks upstream bugs where a negative value reaches ln(), propagating incorrect but plausible-looking results through financial calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike sqrt() which properly validates the sign bit and reverts for negative inputs, ln() completely ignores the sign. If an upstream subtraction produces a slightly negative intermediate value (e.g., negative interest rate or negative price), ln() will return a normal-looking result instead of reverting, allowing the computation to continue with wrong values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ln() extracts mantissa with `and(input, MANTISSA_MASK)` ignoring sign bit at position 240; no sign validation

preconditions: A negative packedFloat is passed to ln()
The calling protocol does not independently validate the input sign

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Sign bit ignored during mantissa extraction; no revert for negative input
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For input = -5.0 (sign bit set, mantissa 5*10^37), ln() computes ln(5) and returns ~1.609, silently ignoring the negative sign
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing check similar to sqrt: `if and(input, MANTISSA_SIGN_MASK) { revert(...) }`

analysis_questions: Does ln() produce an incorrect sign in the result for negative inputs, or always positive?
What happens when ln(negative) result is used in subsequent calculations?
Is there any downstream protocol that could pass a negative value to ln()?
validation_requirements: Call Ln.ln(Float128.toPackedFloat(-5, 0)) and verify it returns ln(5) without reverting
Compare behavior with sqrt which correctly reverts on negative input
Test with various negative values including -1, -0.5, -100

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A protocol computes ln(reserveRatio) where reserveRatio can go negative during extreme market conditions. Instead of reverting and halting the bad-state calculation, ln() returns a valid-looking positive result. The protocol proceeds with a wrong interest rate or price, enabling value extraction by informed actors.
prerequisites: Protocol doesn't independently validate input domain

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect but plausible results propagate through financial formulas, masking upstream errors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users affected by calculations depending on ln() output
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires library fix adding sign validation

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing negative input test for ln()

threats.title: Exponent Underflow in add/sub for Small Raw Exponents Produces Massive Wrong Exponent
threats.hypothesis_description: In add() and sub(), the assembly instruction `r := sub(aExp, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, MAX_DIGITS_M))` performs unsigned subtraction. When the raw exponent value packed in aExp is less than MAX_DIGITS_M (38), this underflows, wrapping to a massive uint256 value. The resulting exponent field is completely wrong, and all subsequent normalization operates on this corrupted value, producing a result with an astronomically incorrect order of magnitude.
threats.investigation_rationale: Numbers with effective exponents below approximately -8154 (raw exponent < 38) for M-size calculations will trigger this underflow. While extreme, these values are within the 14-bit exponent range and could occur in deeply nested calculations or scientific computing contexts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `r := sub(aExp, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, MAX_DIGITS_M))` underflows when raw packed exponent < 38; unsigned wrap produces huge value

preconditions: At least one operand has a very small raw exponent (effective exponent < -8154)
Both operands are M-size and non-zero
The operand with the larger exponent has raw exponent < 38

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsigned subtraction `sub(aExp, shl(242, 38))` wraps for small aExp
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If aExp represents raw exponent 20 (effective -8172), sub produces 2^256 - (38-20)*2^242, completely corrupting the result exponent field
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check on raw exponent before subtraction; should check if raw exponent >= MAX_DIGITS_M

analysis_questions: What is the minimum raw exponent that avoids underflow for M-size?
Can toPackedFloat() create values with raw exponents below the underflow threshold?
Does the L-size branch have the same issue with DIGIT_DIFF_76_L?
validation_requirements: Create packedFloats with very small exponents (raw < 38)
Add two such values and verify the result is corrupted
Test with exponents at the boundary of the valid range

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Adding two extremely small numbers (exponents < -8154) produces a result with a wrapped exponent, appearing as an astronomically large number. If used in a balance or pricing calculation, this could create phantom value.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Completely wrong results for extreme-range arithmetic
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of protocols operating at extreme value ranges

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge-case tests for minimum exponent values

threats.title: sqrt() Heron's Method Branch Exponent Underflow for Small Exponents
threats.hypothesis_description: In the sqrt() Heron's method branch (else path), the assembly code performs `aExp := sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` or `sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M_PLUS_1)`. When the raw exponent is small (below MAX_DIGITS_M=38 or MAX_DIGITS_M_PLUS_1=39), this unsigned subtraction wraps to a huge value. The subsequent `div(sub(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), 2)` performs unsigned division on the wrapped value, producing a completely wrong result exponent.
threats.investigation_rationale: This branch handles numbers with effective exponents below approximately -8154 (when the condition for the 512-bit branch is not met). The unsigned arithmetic on the exponent has no bounds checking, and the wrap-around propagates through the halving and offset operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `aExp := sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` underflows for aExp < 38; then `aExp := add(div(sub(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), 2), ZERO_OFFSET)` operates on wrapped value

preconditions: Input has a very small raw exponent (< 38)
Input enters the Heron's method branch (else path in sqrt)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: aExp wraps to ~2^256 - small_value, then div(sub(huge_value, 8192), 2) produces ~2^255, completely wrong exponent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check ensuring aExp >= MAX_DIGITS_M before subtraction

analysis_questions: Which inputs reach the Heron's method branch with raw exponents < 38?
Does the 512-bit branch condition correctly redirect most small-exponent inputs?
What is the correct behavior for sqrt of numbers near the minimum representable value?
validation_requirements: Construct inputs with very small exponents that enter the Heron's branch
Verify the result exponent is wrapped/incorrect
Compare with expected mathematical result

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Computing sqrt of an extremely small number (e.g., near minimum representable) in the Heron's method branch produces a result with a completely wrong exponent, appearing as a very large number instead of a small one.
prerequisites: Input with effective exponent < -8154 reaching Heron's branch

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect sqrt results for extreme-range inputs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocols using sqrt with potentially very small values

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge-case tests for sqrt with minimum exponent values

threats.title: calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 branches can overflow updatedMantissa (unchecked in assembly), corrupting ln() output and potentially breaking monotonicity
threats.hypothesis_description: Ln.calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 compute updatedMantissa via many additions of fractional terms. Several branches use inline assembly with repeated `add(updatedMantissa, div(mul(k, mantissa), denom))` without overflow checks. For sufficiently large mantissa values, the sum can exceed 2^256-1 and wrap, producing an arbitrary smaller number. This wrapped updatedMantissa is then used to compute z and Taylor terms, yielding corrupted ln outputs.
threats.investigation_rationale: These functions are public and also used internally by ln_helper. Because Solidity 0.8 overflow checks do not apply in Yul, wrapping is feasible. If upstream code can supply non-normalized or oversized mantissas into ln_helper paths, an attacker could create discontinuities/non-monotonicity, undermining any economic formula relying on ln being increasing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: inline assembly repeatedly adds scaled mantissa terms (add/div/mul) without overflow checks; Solidity 0.8 protections not active in Yul

preconditions: Caller can supply a mantissa large enough to overflow 256-bit when combined with multipliers (e.g., mantissa near type(uint256).max or otherwise not constrained to 76-digit scaled bounds)
The overflowed updatedMantissa influences ln() result in a state-changing upstream function
Upstream code does not validate mantissa magnitude or normalization before using Ln functions

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: Ln.sol assembly blocks inside calculateQ2/calculateQ3 (multiple branches)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: wraparound can map a larger input to a smaller updatedMantissa, breaking monotonic behavior and correctness
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no explicit bounds checks on mantissa; no use of addmod with known modulus; no revert on overflow

analysis_questions: What are the maximum possible mantissa values fed into calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 from ln_helper after scaling (should be ~1e76)?
Can malformed packedFloat inputs make uMantissa exceed intended bounds before calling calculateQ1/Q2/Q3?
Do any branches guarantee updatedMantissa <= 2^256-1 for all intended ranges?
validation_requirements: Fuzz calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 with large mantissa values and check for wraparound (updatedMantissa < mantissa unexpectedly)
Instrument ln_helper path to capture uMantissa range before each calculateQ* call
Property test monotonicity: for x>y>0, ensure ln(x)>ln(y) over intended domain; attempt counterexamples via crafted inputs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker passes a crafted packedFloat that causes ln_helper to produce a uMantissa near 2^256-1 (via non-normalized mantissa/exponent). calculateQ2 overflows updatedMantissa, wrapping to a much smaller value; ln() then outputs an unexpectedly low/high number, letting attacker exploit a pricing curve or rate computation that assumes ln monotonicity.
prerequisites: Integration where user can influence packedFloat raw bits or where intermediate computations can exceed intended mantissa bounds

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: If ln() is used in bonding curves/interest models, non-monotonic outputs can enable risk-free arbitrage or bypass of safety thresholds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users interacting with modules relying on ln() for pricing/risk
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires patching library and redeploying/upgrading integrator contracts

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing adversarial fuzzing on raw mantissa ranges and overflow behavior inside Yul

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: single transaction call to ln() or direct calls to calculateQ*
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: detectable by invariant tests comparing against high-precision reference implementation off-chain

threats.title: sqrt(0) Uses stop() Opcode Halting Calling Transaction Without Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: The sqrt() function handles zero input with the EVM `stop()` opcode instead of returning packedFloat(0). `stop()` halts the entire execution context with success status and empty return data. Any state changes made by the calling contract BEFORE the sqrt call are committed, while all code AFTER is silently dropped. The calling contract receives empty return data which decodes to 0 in Solidity, masking the incomplete execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: Any DeFi protocol performing operations like `collectFee(); root = sqrt(value); refundUser()` where value can be zero will have fees collected but refunds never executed. The transaction appears successful on-chain, making the issue extremely hard to diagnose.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Assembly block contains `if iszero(a) { stop() }` which halts EVM execution instead of returning zero

preconditions: A calling contract invokes Float128.sqrt with packedFloat.wrap(0) as input
The calling function has state-modifying operations after the sqrt call

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker triggers any protocol code path where sqrt receives zero input
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: State changes before sqrt committed, changes after dropped. Partial execution creates inconsistent state exploitable for fund extraction.

economic_questions: Which integrating protocols call sqrt() in a context where input could be zero?
What state changes occur before vs after sqrt calls in integrating protocols?
Can an attacker force the sqrt input to zero via market manipulation?
validation_requirements: Confirm stop() behavior in the calling contract context via forge test
Verify that Solidity handles empty returndata as packedFloat(0)
Map state change ordering in known integrators

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Variable - depends on how to force sqrt(0) in target protocol
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.001 ETH gas cost for single transaction
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to full value of state changes after sqrt call that are never executed
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially infinite ROI if fees are collected but refunds dropped

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When integrating protocol has high-value operations straddling sqrt call

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users of any protocol calling Float128.sqrt with potentially zero input
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full value of post-sqrt operations that are silently dropped

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace EVM execution with stop() in calling contract

threats.title: All Comparison Functions Fail for Mathematically Equal Values with Different M/L Representations
threats.hypothesis_description: eq() uses raw bitwise comparison, while le() and ge() use strict lt/gt instead of lte/gte in the equal-exponent body. When the same mathematical value is represented in M-size (38 digits) and L-size (72 digits), ALL five comparison functions (eq, lt, gt, le, ge) return false. This violates trichotomy: no ordering relationship holds between mathematically equal values. Different arithmetic operations naturally produce different M/L encodings for the same value based on the MAXIMUM_EXPONENT auto-scaling threshold.
threats.investigation_rationale: Protocols using these comparisons for liquidation thresholds, balance checks, invariant validation, or access control on numeric bounds will have incorrect behavior. A value exactly at a threshold would bypass ALL conditional checks, as it's simultaneously not less than, not equal to, and not greater than the threshold.

proof_of_signal.pattern: eq() at line ~475 uses `packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)` (bitwise only); le() at ~512 uses `retVal := lt(aMan, bMan)` (strict); ge() at ~570 uses `lt(bMan, aMan)` (strict) — all miss equality for M-vs-L encoded same values

preconditions: Two packedFloat values represent the same mathematical number
One is M-encoded (38 digits) and the other is L-encoded (72 digits)
This naturally occurs when values are computed via different arithmetic paths

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: eq: `retVal = packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)` — bitwise comparison; le: `retVal := lt(aMan, bMan)` — strict less-than in equal-exponent branch; ge: `retVal := lt(bMan, aMan)` — strict less-than in equal-exponent branch
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker computes a value via a path that produces M-encoding while the protocol's threshold is L-encoded (or vice versa), bypassing the threshold check entirely
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Bypass liquidation boundaries, safety checks, or balance invariants by exploiting the fact that equal values are invisible to all comparisons

economic_questions: Which arithmetic operations produce M vs L for the same mathematical value?
Can an attacker control which code path is used to compute a value?
What protocol invariants rely on these comparison functions?
validation_requirements: Create two packedFloats representing 1.0 in M and L, verify all 5 comparisons return false
Identify arithmetic sequences that naturally produce M vs L for same value
Test le/ge with same-exponent same-mantissa values after L-normalization

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal - requires only crafting inputs that produce M/L divergence
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to value protected by the bypassed threshold check

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When target protocol has values near M/L auto-scaling threshold (MAXIMUM_EXPONENT = -18)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users of protocols relying on Float128 comparisons for safety checks
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full value at risk behind the bypassed comparison
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Protocol TVL if comparison guards solvency invariants

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test toPackedFloat(1,0) vs mul(toPackedFloat(10,0), toPackedFloat(1,-1)) for M/L divergence

threats.title: sdiv Sign Error and exp() Overflow in add/sub Corrupts Results for Exponent Differences ≥ 115
threats.hypothesis_description: In add() and sub(), when aligning mantissas of operands with different exponents, the code uses `sdiv(bMan, exp(BASE, adj))`. Two distinct bugs exist: (1) At adj=77, exp(10,77) ≈ 10^77 > 2^255, so sdiv interprets the denominator as negative, producing a wrong-sign result. (2) At adj≥78, exp(10,adj) overflows uint256, producing a modular-wrapped garbage denominator. Both corrupt the addition/subtraction result. The exponent difference threshold for M-size numbers is only 115 (adj = exponent_diff - 38), which is well within the documented valid range of [-3000, +3000].
threats.investigation_rationale: Adding a large number (e.g., exponent +100) to a small number (e.g., exponent -100) — a common operation in financial calculations — produces silently corrupted results. The corrupted value propagates through subsequent calculations, potentially causing incorrect pricing, balance tracking, or solvency assessments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `bMan := sdiv(bMan, exp(BASE, adj))` — sdiv interprets exp(10,77) as negative (>2^255), and exp(10, adj>=78) overflows uint256 producing garbage divisor

preconditions: Two operands with raw exponent difference > 115 for M-size numbers (adj > 77)
Both operands are within the documented valid range [-3000, +3000]
The operands are not both zero

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: exp(10, 77) = 10^77 > 2^255, triggering sdiv sign misinterpretation; exp(10, 78+) overflows uint256
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Provide two valid packedFloat values with exponent difference > 115 to any protocol function using Float128.add/sub
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Corrupted arithmetic result used in share/asset conversion, pricing, or accounting produces incorrect balances extractable by attacker

economic_questions: What is the exact corrupted value for specific input pairs?
How does sdiv sign error at adj=77 differ from exp overflow at adj=78+?
Can the corruption consistently favor the attacker?
validation_requirements: Test add(toPackedFloat(1, 100), toPackedFloat(1, -100)) and verify corruption
Verify sdiv(positive, exp(10,77)) produces wrong-sign result
Quantify magnitude of error for different exponent differences

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Variable — depends on how to force large exponent differences in target protocol
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Result corruption can be orders of magnitude, potentially unbounded profit
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially very high if corrupted value is used in token minting or fund release

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When protocol processes values with large magnitude differences

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users of protocols where add/sub inputs can have >115 exponent difference
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete corruption of the arithmetic result
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Protocol TVL if accounting relies on corrupted results

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: adj threshold: 77 for sdiv sign bug, 78 for exp overflow; corresponds to exponent difference of 115/116
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test exp(10,77) > 2^255; test sdiv(10^38, exp(10,77)) produces negative

threats.title: Normalization Invariant Violation Produces Denormalized packedFloat Propagating Through Calculations
threats.hypothesis_description: The normalization logic in add/sub has a complex set of assembly branches for determining M vs L output and handling digit overflow. The L-flag must be set if and only if the mantissa has 72 digits. If an edge case in the normalization produces a mantissa with the wrong number of digits relative to the L-flag (e.g., 72-digit mantissa without L-flag, or 38-digit with L-flag), all subsequent arithmetic operations will apply incorrect scaling factors, producing silently wrong results that compound through chains of calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The normalization code paths in add() and sub() are the most complex parts of the library with numerous assembly branches. A single edge case producing an L-flag/mantissa mismatch would corrupt all downstream computations. The MAXIMUM_EXPONENT threshold determines M/L auto-scaling and is a critical decision boundary.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Normalization logic with multiple assembly branches determining _isM flag, MANTISSA_L_FLAG_MASK, and digit reduction — complex state machine with no final assertion that mantissa matches L-flag

preconditions: Specific input values that hit an edge case in the normalization branches
The result's effective exponent is near the MAXIMUM_EXPONENT boundary (-18)
Addition or subtraction produces a result near the M/L transition boundary

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No final validation that mantissa digit count matches L-flag after normalization
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Craft inputs near the M/L scaling boundary to produce denormalized intermediate results
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Denormalized result passed to mul/div applies wrong 10^34 scaling factor, creating 34 orders of magnitude error

economic_questions: Are there specific input pairs that produce L-flag/mantissa mismatches?
How does a denormalized result propagate through subsequent mul/div operations?
What is the magnitude of error when wrong scaling is applied?
validation_requirements: Fuzz add/sub with inputs near MAXIMUM_EXPONENT boundary
Validate L-flag consistency for all outputs: if L set, MIN_L <= mantissa <= MAX_L
Test chains of operations: add → mul → div with boundary inputs

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 10^34 magnitude error in result
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially astronomical if 34-order magnitude error exploitable

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When values near the M/L boundary are processed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users of protocols where denormalized values enter calculations
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Protocol TVL if accounting uses corrupted values

threats.title: ln() Recursive Call May Exceed Gas Limit for Crafted Inputs Near Reciprocal Precision Boundary
threats.hypothesis_description: The ln() function handles inputs > 1.0 by computing the reciprocal and recursively calling ln(). The recursion should be exactly depth 1 — the reciprocal should always take the non-recursive branch. However, if a precision error in the reciprocal computation produces a value that still triggers the recursive branch (positiveExp < MAX_DIGITS_L for L or MAX_DIGITS_M for M), infinite recursion occurs until gas runs out. This creates a denial-of-service for any protocol operation depending on ln().
threats.investigation_rationale: The recursion termination depends on the reciprocal's exponent being sufficiently negative. If there's a narrow range of inputs where floating-point imprecision in the reciprocal shifts the exponent just enough to retrigger the recursive path, any protocol calling ln() with such inputs would have all transactions revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ln_helper first branch computes reciprocal then calls `ln(toPackedFloat(...))` recursively — termination depends on reciprocal having positiveExp >= MAX_DIGITS in the recursive call

preconditions: Input to ln() has a positive effective exponent (number > 1.0)
The reciprocal computation produces a value with positiveExp < MAX_DIGITS_L/M
This causes the recursive call to also take the first branch (another reciprocal + recursion)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: one_over_argument computed via integer division may have precision error affecting exponent classification
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Provide specific inputs to ln() that cause infinite recursion via precision-boundary reciprocal
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS on any protocol function that calls ln() with the triggering input, preventing liquidations or other time-sensitive operations

economic_questions: Is there any input where the reciprocal's exponent falls in the recursive branch range?
What is the gas cost of the recursion before it fails?
Can an attacker force a protocol to call ln() with a triggering input?
validation_requirements: Fuzz ln() with inputs having positive exponents to find recursion triggers
Trace reciprocal computation to verify exponent always stays in non-recursive range
Test with inputs near mantissa boundaries (MIN_M_DIGIT_NUMBER, MAX_L_DIGIT_NUMBER)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Variable — depends on how to force ln() call with triggering input
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Full gas limit wasted on failed transaction
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect profit from blocked liquidations or time-sensitive operations
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: As long as the DoS condition persists

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When blocking ln() would prevent liquidations at profit

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users of protocols depending on ln() for the blocked operation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value at risk from delayed/blocked liquidations

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Recursion boundary: positiveExp vs MAX_DIGITS_L (72) and MAX_DIGITS_M (38)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify reciprocal exponent for all valid positive-exponent inputs

threats.title: Exponent Arithmetic Underflow in Core Operations Causes Massive Value Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: Exponent arithmetic in `mul` and `div` lacks bounds checking. When operations result in an exponent below the minimum representable value (-8192), the 14-bit raw exponent field silently underflows and wraps around to a large positive value.
threats.investigation_rationale: This allows an attacker to convert microscopic dust values into astronomically large values, breaking protocol solvency.

proof_of_signal.pattern: rExp := sub(add(shr(EXPONENT_BIT, aExp), shr(EXPONENT_BIT, bExp)), ZERO_OFFSET) without underflow checks

preconditions: Protocol allows users to input or accumulate tiny dust values
Protocol multiplies or divides these values using Float128

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rExp := sub(..., ZERO_OFFSET) wraps to positive on underflow
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker supplies dust values (e.g., 10^-8000) to trigger underflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Dust value inflates to 10^+8000, allowing attacker to mint infinite shares or drain pools

economic_questions: Which protocol integration points allow multiplication of user-controlled dust values?
validation_requirements: Test mul() with two inputs having exponents near -8192 to confirm positive wraparound

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets managed by integrating protocol

threats.title: Non-Canonical Zero Inputs Wipe Out Protocol Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: The `add` and `sub` functions check for zero using strict bitwise equality, failing to catch non-canonical zeros (mantissa = 0, large exponent). When added to a valid balance, the exponent alignment shifts the valid balance right by a massive factor, reducing it to zero.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can permanently destroy protocol accounting balances by depositing or transferring a crafted non-canonical zero.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (packedFloat.unwrap(a) == 0) return b;

preconditions: Protocol accepts packedFloat inputs directly from users
Protocol adds user input to global state balances

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: bMan := sdiv(bMan, exp(BASE, adj)) where adj is massive
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker submits packedFloat with mantissa 0 and exponent 16383
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol balance becomes 0, locking all user funds (griefing) or allowing attacker to manipulate share ratios

economic_questions: Can the attacker profit from a zeroed global balance, e.g., by minting new shares at a favorable ratio?
validation_requirements: Test add() with a non-canonical zero and a normal value to confirm the normal value is wiped

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Primarily griefing, potential for share manipulation profit

threats.title: Implicit Mantissa Size Assumption in decode() Causes Silent Integer Overflow
threats.hypothesis_description: `decode()` returns the raw mantissa without indicating whether it is M-size (38 digits) or L-size (72 digits). Integrating protocols that expect standard ~38-digit precision will multiply this 72-digit mantissa with other values, causing a silent `uint256` overflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: Attackers can force an L-size representation to trigger overflows in the integrating protocol's accounting, leading to corrupted balances or prices.
deployment_assumptions: Integrating protocol uses decode() and performs multiplication on the returned mantissa without checking its size
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence, depends on upstream protocol integration

proof_of_signal.pattern: mantissa := and(float, MANTISSA_MASK) returned without L-flag context

preconditions: Protocol uses decode() to extract mantissa for standard math
Attacker can supply or generate an L-size packedFloat

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker provides L-size input, causing protocol's internal multiplication to silently overflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Overflow results in artificially low debt or artificially high collateral value

economic_questions: How do integrating protocols handle the dual-size nature of the decoded mantissa?
validation_requirements: Review upstream protocol code for unchecked math on decode() outputs

threats.title: Unchecked Exponent Bounds in toPackedFloat Allows Arbitrary Value Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: `toPackedFloat` does not validate that the provided exponent fits within the 14-bit exponent field. If `exponent + ZERO_OFFSET` is negative, the 2's complement representation is shifted left by 242 bits, overwriting the exponent field with all 1s (16383).
threats.investigation_rationale: This silently converts a microscopic value (e.g., 10^-8193) into an astronomically large value (10^+8191), allowing attackers to inflate their shares or rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: float := or(float, or(mantissa, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, add(exponent, ZERO_OFFSET)))) without bounds check

preconditions: Protocol uses toPackedFloat to encode user-influenced or dynamically calculated values
Attacker can manipulate the calculation to produce an exponent < -8192

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Negative exponent addition underflows and fills top bits with 1s
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker forces protocol to encode a value with exponent -8193
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Value is encoded as 10^+8191, granting the attacker massive artificial balance

economic_questions: Which protocol functions dynamically calculate exponents and pass them to toPackedFloat?
validation_requirements: Test toPackedFloat(1, -8193) to confirm it returns a massive positive packedFloat

threats.title: Unbounded/unsafe integer exponentiation in digit counting can revert or overflow in ln Taylor pre-processing, creating transaction-level DoS for integrators
threats.hypothesis_description: Ln.intermediateTermAddition computes z_int and then rescales it using `10 ** uint(diff * -1)` or division by `10 ** uint(diff)`. If diff becomes large (via malformed mantissa inputs that break assumptions about digit lengths), `10 ** n` can overflow and revert in Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic. This can make ln() unusable for certain crafted inputs, causing a revert in any protocol operation that depends on ln().
threats.investigation_rationale: intermediateTermAddition derives `len_z_int` via findNumberOfDigits and expects values near 10^76 scaling. If mantissa is malformed (not normalized or out of expected range), z_int and len_z_int can diverge, producing large |diff| and triggering overflow in exponentiation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: z_int = diff < 0 ? int(uint(z_int) * 10 ** uint(diff * -1)) : int(uint(z_int) / 10 ** uint(diff));

preconditions: Integrator calls Ln.ln() on attacker-influenced packedFloat inputs
Attacker can supply non-normalized mantissa/exponent such that mantissa passed into intermediateTermAddition yields extreme len_z_int or diff
Protocol operation does not catch/handle ln() revert (most don't)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Solidity exponentiation on potentially attacker-influenced exponent (diff) without explicit bounds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time ln() is invoked on attacker-controlled inputs

operational_questions: Can malformed packedFloat inputs reach the Taylor-series path (else branch) with mantissa far from expected range?
What are maximum observed |diff| values under fuzzing malformed mantissas?
Does the integrating protocol have alternate code paths if ln() reverts (fallback pricing, pause)?
validation_requirements: Fuzz ln() with random packedFloat.wrap(uint256) inputs; classify reverts and identify overflow in `10 ** ...`
Add runtime guards: clamp diff to safe bounds or pre-canonicalize mantissa/exponent before ln
Measure DoS impact: which state-changing functions depend on ln and would revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify protocol call path that must call ln() (e.g., trade/quote/update)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft packedFloat input that routes into intermediateTermAddition with extreme z_int scaling requirements
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger overflow/revert in `10 ** uint(...)` leading to full transaction revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat to block protocol operations that depend on ln() (user-level DoS)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: blocks specific operations that call ln(); can become high if ln is on a critical path
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to medium (requires patching integrator or adding input validation)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any operation depending on ln computation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated reverted transactions with identical crafted packedFloat values
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden spike in reverts in ln-dependent functions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: High-frequency calls to a single endpoint with varying packedFloat inputs

threats.title: Lack of input normalization enforcement allows attacker-crafted packedFloat bit patterns to corrupt arithmetic outputs used in upstream accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: Float128.decode() and Ln.calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 accept raw integers and packedFloat values without enforcing the library’s normalization invariants (38/72 digits mantissa, consistent L-flag). If an integrating protocol accepts user-supplied packedFloat or stores intermediate results without canonicalization, an attacker can introduce malformed values that cause silent overflows/underflows in assembly arithmetic and return plausible but incorrect results, potentially manipulating prices, shares, or risk metrics.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A highlights malformed input assumptions and lack of sanitization as a primary trust-boundary risk. Even if core library intends callers to use toPackedFloat, nothing enforces it at the type level.
deployment_assumptions: At least one integrating contract accepts packedFloat values from untrusted users or external protocols without canonicalization/validation
Or, integrating code can persist malformed packedFloat in storage and reuse it later
threats.config_evidence: No deployment/integration code provided in scope; must be confirmed in Phase D by reviewing call sites and input validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Assumes packedFloat inputs normalized; no validation; attacker can craft packedFloat.wrap(uint256) to trigger silent incorrect arithmetic

preconditions: An upstream contract uses these libraries and does not strictly enforce normalization invariants on all packedFloat boundaries (inputs and persisted state)
Attacker can provide or influence packedFloat bit patterns (including L-flag mismatch with mantissa digits)
Upstream uses returned values in state-changing arithmetic (mint/burn/transfer accounting, pricing, risk checks)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: User-defined value type packedFloat can be wrapped from arbitrary uint256; library functions do not validate mantissa digit count or exponent bounds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Protocol-specific; whenever untrusted packedFloat can reach core math

operational_questions: Do any external/public functions in integrators accept packedFloat directly?
Are packedFloat values ever decoded/encoded across contract boundaries without canonicalization?
Are there invariants checked in integrators to enforce mantissa digit ranges and L-flag consistency?
validation_requirements: Integration audit: identify all entry points taking packedFloat or raw uint256 later wrapped to packedFloat
Runtime assertions/fuzzing: enforce mantissa digit bounds and verify failures for malformed inputs
Economic tests: attempt to bias share/price calculations with malformed packedFloat that still passes type checks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Locate an entry point where attacker can influence a packedFloat used in accounting
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft malformed encoding (e.g., set L-flag with 38-digit mantissa, or set exponent bits out of expected range)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger arithmetic in upstream (add/mul/div or ln reductions) that produces silently incorrect result
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit miscalculation to extract value (mint extra shares, underpay fees, bypass liquidation bounds)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium to high depending on integration: mispricing, accounting corruption, potential insolvency
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any math-dependent economic path in integrators

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate (crafting bit patterns and targeting a vulnerable integrator path)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusual packedFloat encodings with inconsistent L-flag/mantissa digit ranges
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Divergence between expected and computed values at boundaries
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated probing with crafted uint256 payloads

threats.title: Value Inflation via div() Exponent Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: In `div()`, the exponent calculation `rExp = (aExp + ZERO_OFFSET) - bExp` is performed inside an `unchecked` block. If a small number is divided by a large number, this underflows, producing a massive exponent instead of a small one.
threats.investigation_rationale: Dividing a small number by a large number should yield a very small number. The underflow causes it to yield a massive number. If a protocol divides a dust balance by a large total supply to compute a share ratio, the attacker gets a massive amount of shares.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { rExp = (aExp + ZERO_OFFSET) - bExp; } underflows

preconditions: Integrating protocol divides a small value by a large value
Attacker can trigger this division

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker divides dust by a large number.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker receives massively inflated shares or assets.

economic_questions: Where does the protocol use `div()`?
Can the numerator be small and denominator large?
validation_requirements: Test `div()` with small numerator and large denominator.

threats.title: calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 Truncation Error Accumulation Could Break ln() Monotonicity at Partition Boundaries
threats.hypothesis_description: The calculateQ1(), calculateQ2(), and calculateQ3() functions approximate multipliers (1.1^q1, 1.014^q2, 1.0013^q3) using digit-by-digit integer arithmetic. Each term `(k * mantissa) / divisor` incurs truncation error of up to (divisor-1). With up to 28 such terms in calculateQ3 q3=7, the accumulated truncation error could reach hundreds of units. At partition boundaries (e.g., mantissa just above vs just below 9860*10^72 in Q2), two very close inputs select different q values, apply different multiplier approximations, and proceed through different Taylor series computations. If the truncation errors are asymmetric across the boundary, the ln() output could violate monotonicity: x > y but ln(x) < ln(y).
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation acknowledges 99 ULP tolerance for ln(). The digit-by-digit approximation method inherently accumulates rounding errors. At partition boundaries where the approximation path changes discontinuously, the error profiles on each side differ, creating a risk of non-monotonic output. A monotonicity violation in ln() directly enables risk-free arbitrage on logarithmic bonding curves.

proof_of_signal.pattern: calculateQ1 q1=7 branch has 8 truncation-prone divisions; calculateQ3 q3=7 has 28 truncation-prone divisions; boundary at mantissa == 9860*10^72 switches between q2=0 and q2=1

preconditions: Two inputs x, y where x > y but both are near a partition boundary
x and y select different q values (e.g., different q2 paths)
Truncation error asymmetry causes ln(x) < ln(y)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Each `div(mul(k, mantissa), 10^n)` truncates up to 10^n - 1 units; 28 terms accumulate errors
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker identifies specific values near partition boundaries where monotonicity fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: On logarithmic bonding curve: buy at lower price, sell at higher price for same underlying movement

economic_questions: What is the maximum accumulated truncation error in calculateQ3 for the q3=7 branch?
Do the partition boundaries have continuous output when switching between q paths?
Is the 99 ULP tolerance sufficient to prevent monotonicity violations at boundaries?
validation_requirements: Compute ln() for values straddling each partition boundary
Verify monotonicity: ln(boundary + epsilon) > ln(boundary - epsilon)
Measure actual ULP error at each boundary

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Protocol-dependent — capital for bonding curve trade
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Small per trade but repeatable if monotonicity is violated

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient liquidity on ln-based bonding curve
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time — monotonicity violation is deterministic

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users of protocols with ln-based pricing
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative drain from bonding curve reserves

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test ln() at all 24 partition boundary values across Q1/Q2/Q3
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Binary search for monotonicity violations near each boundary

threats.title: intermediateTermAddition Taylor Series z_int Truncation Causes Excessive Error for ln() Near 1.0
threats.hypothesis_description: In intermediateTermAddition(), the variable z_int (representing 1 - normalized_mantissa) is truncated from up to 76 digits to exactly 38 digits via `z_int = diff < 0 ? int(uint(z_int) * 10^uint(diff*-1)) : int(uint(z_int) / 10^uint(diff))`. This truncation loses up to 38 digits of precision before the Taylor series even begins. The subsequent 15-term Taylor series uses 38-digit packedFloat arithmetic, and each of the 14 multiplication+division+addition steps compounds rounding errors. For z near its maximum (~0.014), the total accumulated error could significantly exceed the stated 99 ULP tolerance, especially for inputs in the (1.0, 1.1) range that the documentation flags as problematic.
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly acknowledges poor precision in the (1.0, 1.1) range, with errors potentially reaching 199 ULP for 38-digit inputs and >50% relative error for extreme cases. The z_int truncation to 38 digits is a clear precision bottleneck that could produce economically significant errors in pricing calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: z_int truncated from up to 76 digits to 38 digits at line 144; Taylor series z^j/j iterated 15 times with 38-digit packedFloat arithmetic accumulating rounding at each step

preconditions: Input to ln() is in the (1.0, 1.1) range
Input uses M-size (38-digit) encoding
z value is near maximum after argument reduction (~0.014)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: z_int truncation at line 144 loses up to 38 significant digits; 14 subsequent mul+div+add operations each add ~1 ULP error
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker chooses inputs in (1.0, 1.1) range where ln error is maximized
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Interest rate or pricing calculation based on ln(1+r) has significant error for small r values near partition boundaries

economic_questions: What is the actual worst-case error for ln() in the (1.0, 1.1) range?
Can the error direction be predicted (always over or under)?
What DeFi applications use ln(1+r) for small r?
validation_requirements: Compute ln() for systematic inputs in (1.0, 1.1) and compare against reference implementation
Measure maximum ULP error and verify against 99 ULP tolerance
Identify specific inputs where error exceeds tolerance

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to error magnitude and protocol TVL

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None for the precision bug itself
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When protocol computes ln(1+r) for small r

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users of protocols using ln() for interest/pricing near 1.0
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Small per transaction, cumulative over time
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Proportional to protocol TVL and ln() usage frequency

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test ln(1.01), ln(1.05), ln(1.09) with both M and L inputs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare against known reference values with arbitrary precision

threats.title: finalTermAddition Accumulated Error Propagation Through Five Sequential Float128 Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: The finalTermAddition() function computes the final ln() result through a chain of five sequential Float128 operations: result + k*ln2 + q1*ln1.1 + q2*lnB + q3*lnC - m10*ln10, with a final multiplication by -1. Each add/mul operation introduces up to 1 ULP rounding error. With 5 additions, 5 multiplications, and the final sign multiplication (11 operations total), the accumulated error could be up to 11 ULP from this function alone, on top of errors from the Taylor series computation. For the (1.0, 1.1) input range where z is already imprecise, this accumulation could push total error well beyond the 99 ULP tolerance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The sequential chain of Float128 operations without intermediate precision boosting represents a systematic error accumulation path. Combined with precision loss in intermediateTermAddition, the final result may have errors exceeding stated tolerances for specific input ranges.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Five sequential add/mul chains: firstTerm = result + k*ln2; secondTerm = firstTerm + q1*ln1.1; etc. — each step introduces rounding; final mul by -1 at line 184

preconditions: ln() called with input where all of k, q1, q2, q3, m10 are non-zero
Taylor series result already has significant error
Final combination amplifies error beyond tolerance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: 11 Float128 operations × 1 ULP each = 11 ULP minimum from finalTermAddition alone
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Select inputs maximizing error in Taylor series AND final term addition
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Systematic bias in ln() error direction enables consistent over/under-pricing

economic_questions: Is the error accumulation additive or does cancellation reduce it?
Does the error have a consistent directional bias?
What input ranges maximize total accumulated error?
validation_requirements: Measure error contribution of each step in finalTermAddition independently
Compute total error for worst-case inputs
Compare against 99 ULP tolerance

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Deterministic — same input always produces same error

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Inputs where k=3, q1=7, q2=7, q3=7 (maximum number of terms)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare full ln() output against arbitrary-precision reference

threats.title: ln() Recursive Call May Not Terminate for Specific Crafted Inputs
threats.hypothesis_description: The ln_helper() function recursively calls ln() when the input's positive exponent indicates a value > 1.0 (first branch). The recursion assumes that computing the reciprocal (one_over_arguments_76) produces a value <= 1.0 that takes the non-recursive second branch. If precision errors in the reciprocal computation or edge cases in the toPackedFloat encoding cause the recursive argument to still appear > 1.0, infinite recursion ensues until gas is exhausted.
threats.investigation_rationale: The reciprocal is computed via integer division and encoded via toPackedFloat. If the input is very close to 1.0 but > 1.0, the reciprocal is close to but < 1.0, and its exponent should make it take the else branch. But if precision in the reciprocal causes it to appear >= 1.0 in the encoded form, the recursion doesn't terminate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Recursive `ln()` call in ln_helper first branch; termination depends on reciprocal value always entering second branch

preconditions: Input value > 1.0 that triggers the first branch
The reciprocal computation produces a value that re-triggers the first branch
This would require a precision error in the reciprocal or edge case in encoding

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Recursive call to ln() with computed reciprocal; no recursion depth limit
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No explicit guard ensuring recursion depth = 1; relies on mathematical property that may not hold for all encoded values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No recursion depth counter or explicit check that recursive argument is < 1.0

analysis_questions: For which inputs does the reciprocal computation in ln_helper first branch produce a value with positiveExp < MAX_DIGITS?
Can the reciprocal of a value slightly > 1.0 be encoded with a positive effective exponent?
Is there a mathematical proof that the recursion always terminates in exactly 1 step?
validation_requirements: Test ln() with values very close to 1.0 (e.g., 1 + 10^-37)
Test ln() with very large values (e.g., 10^3000)
Monitor recursion depth for a wide range of inputs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If an input exists that causes infinite recursion, any protocol calling ln() on that input would have the transaction revert with out-of-gas. If ln() is used in a critical path (e.g., liquidation calculation), this creates a denial-of-service preventing the protocol operation.
prerequisites: Specific input value that causes reciprocal to re-trigger first branch

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS on protocol operations depending on ln()
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of affected protocol operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Protocol must avoid the triggering input value

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing recursion depth and termination tests

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas consumption of recursive ln() calls
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via gas exhaustion in transaction

threats.title: sub(0, b) Returns Non-Canonical Representation When b Has Non-Canonical Form
threats.hypothesis_description: In sub(), when a=0 and b is non-zero, the code XORs the sign bit: `b := xor(MANTISSA_SIGN_MASK, b)`. If b is a non-canonical packedFloat (e.g., zero mantissa with non-zero exponent/flags), the XOR produces another non-canonical value that propagates through the system. Additionally, if b itself was obtained from a buggy operation that produced a non-canonical form, sub(0, b) preserves and propagates the corruption with only a sign flip.
threats.investigation_rationale: The documentation states zero must be represented as all-zero bits. But if any code path (including external callers using packedFloat.wrap) creates a non-canonical zero, sub(0, nonCanonicalZero) returns the non-canonical zero with flipped sign. This non-canonical value then fails comparison checks against canonical zero.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `gt(b, 0)` checks raw uint256, not mantissa; XOR sign on any non-zero packed value including non-canonical zeros

preconditions: First operand is zero (canonical)
Second operand is a non-canonical packedFloat (e.g., zero mantissa but non-zero exponent bits)
Non-canonical value was created by external code or propagated from a normalization bug

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: `gt(b, 0)` is true for any non-zero uint256, even if mantissa field is 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A packedFloat with mantissa=0 but exponent bits set passes the gt check; XOR produces non-canonical negative-zero-with-exponent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should check mantissa field specifically, or validate that b represents a valid non-zero number

analysis_questions: Can any internal Float128 operation produce a non-canonical zero (mantissa=0, other bits set)?
If so, does sub(0, nonCanonicalZero) propagate the corruption?
Do comparison functions handle non-canonical zeros correctly?
validation_requirements: Create a non-canonical zero via packedFloat.wrap and test sub(0, nonCanonicalZero)
Verify the result is non-canonical
Test if the result behaves correctly in subsequent comparisons and arithmetic

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a protocol constructs packedFloats directly via wrap (or if a normalization bug produces non-canonical zeros), sub(0, value) propagates the non-canonical form. Subsequent eq(result, 0) returns false, potentially causing balance checks to fail or invariant violations.
prerequisites: Non-canonical packedFloat exists in the system

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Propagation of corrupted values through arithmetic chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users whose operations encounter non-canonical values
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires library fix adding mantissa validation

threats.title: Types.sol packedFloat is a raw uint256 wrapper; lack of constructor/validation enables forged bitpatterns that violate canonical invariants in downstream protocols
threats.hypothesis_description: packedFloat is a user-defined value type over uint256 with no enforced invariants at the type level. Attackers can forge arbitrary bit patterns via packedFloat.wrap() in any integrating contract, including non-canonical zeros, inconsistent L-flag/mantissa sizes, and out-of-range exponent encodings. While the library attempts to operate on assumed-normalized inputs, malformed values can trigger silent wrong math and inconsistent comparisons in protocols that accept packedFloat from untrusted callers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A identifies malformed input as the primary trust-boundary risk. Even if Float128/Ln functions are internally used, external protocols may expose packedFloat as an input type. The threat is configuration/integration dependent but practical in systems that treat packedFloat as a safe numeric type.
deployment_assumptions: An integrating contract exposes functions taking packedFloat as user input or accepts packedFloat values from untrusted contracts
Integrator does not canonicalize/validate packedFloat inputs with toPackedFloat or invariant checks before use
threats.config_evidence: Only the library/type definition is provided; no downstream validation patterns are shown.

proof_of_signal.pattern: type packedFloat is uint256; no validation/constructors; arbitrary wrap() possible in downstream code

preconditions: Downstream contracts treat packedFloat as an externally supplied parameter type
Attacker can call those downstream functions and pass packedFloat.wrap(arbitrary_uint256)
Downstream uses Float128/Ln operations assuming normalization/canonical zero

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: raw uint256 wrapper with no invariant enforcement
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: forged bitpatterns bypass semantic assumptions (normalized mantissa, canonical zero, valid exponent range)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no type-level validation; relies entirely on caller discipline

analysis_questions: Do any internal library functions rely on canonical zero checks (unwrap==0) that malformed zeros bypass?
Which malformed encodings cause the most dangerous downstream misbehavior (comparisons, scaling, division-by-zero paths)?
Should the broader system provide a validation helper (e.g., isValidPackedFloat) to guard entry points?
validation_requirements: Create a corpus of malformed packedFloat bit patterns (non-canonical zero, L-flag mismatch, exponent extremes) and run through key operations
Demonstrate a plausible downstream integration misuse scenario (e.g., using lt/le for limits) and show bypass with forged encoding

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: In a protocol that accepts packedFloat inputs (e.g., user sets a limit/price as packedFloat), attacker passes a forged encoding with mantissa=0 but non-zero exponent/flags. The protocol may accept it as '0' after decode-like processing, but Float128 comparisons and arithmetic treat it as non-zero (unwrap != 0), enabling bypass or causing miscalculations that can be exploited depending on the surrounding logic.
prerequisites: A downstream protocol exposes packedFloat inputs without validation
Attacker understands encoding and crafts payloads

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol-specific; can range from DoS to mispricing and fund loss depending on integration
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of protocols integrating packedFloat as an input type
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Add strict input validation/canonicalization at integration boundaries

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing adversarial-input tests in integrator context for packedFloat.wrap()-crafted values

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: property tests on invariants for arbitrary wrapped values

threats.title: External-call surface expansion: Ln.calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 are public and may be abused for gas-griefing within aggregators
threats.hypothesis_description: Ln exposes calculateQ1, calculateQ2, and calculateQ3 as public pure functions with large branching/assembly arithmetic. While they do not change state, protocols that integrate Ln as an external contract/library wrapper (e.g., via delegatecall or a predeploy math contract) might inadvertently expose these as callable endpoints and include them in multicall/aggregator workflows. Attackers could spam calls to increase node load or cause out-of-gas failures in batched calls that include these functions.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is operationally relevant when these libraries are deployed as callable contracts (common for shared math libraries on some stacks) or wrapped behind public endpoints; DoS via gas grief is realistic at the infrastructure/application layer even without on-chain state changes.
deployment_assumptions: Project deploys Ln as a standalone callable contract or exposes it through a router/multicall that users can invoke
Integrator includes these calls in bundled operations where a revert/OG affects stateful steps
threats.config_evidence: No deployment scripts provided showing whether Ln is deployed as a callable contract; requires Phase D deployment review.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function calculateQ2(uint256 mantissa) public pure returns (...) { ... large assembly blocks ... } (public visibility)

preconditions: Ln is deployed/exposed as an externally callable contract endpoint (not just linked library code)
There exists an operational workflow where third-party calls to these endpoints consume shared gas budget or cause batch failures
Attackers can economically justify spamming calls (low cost / high impact on service)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public pure functions with heavy branching/assembly can be used as cheap grief targets when exposed behind shared-call infrastructure.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous, if endpoint is public and monitored services are sensitive

operational_questions: Is Ln deployed as a contract on-chain or only linked as a Solidity library?
Are there routers/multicalls that forward arbitrary calls to Ln?
Do any off-chain services (keepers/relayers) batch calls including Ln functions and fail if one call consumes too much gas?
validation_requirements: Review deployment artifacts for Ln bytecode deployment
Benchmark gas usage for calculateQ1/Q2/Q3 worst-case branches and assess batch failure risk

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Confirm Ln is callable externally (deployed address).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft inputs that take the most expensive code paths (largest branch sequences).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Spam calls or embed them in batch calls to cause consistent OOG in shared workflows.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit operational disruption (failed batch executions, increased infra costs).

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Service degradation / batch execution failures; increased operational costs
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to Medium (rate limiting / endpoint isolation)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Relayer/keeper/multicall workflows if they include these calls

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High frequency calls to Ln contract addresses with varying mantissa values
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Spikes in RPC CPU usage correlated with these selectors
